2.5 BRAKE SYSTEM
2.5.1 FRONT DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
2.5.2 REAR DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
2.5.3 TROUBLESHOOTING
2.5.4 BRAKE PADS FRONT / REAR
2.5.5 BRAKE CALIPER & PISTON
NOTES :- 164
2.5.1 FRONT DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
FRONT DISC BAKE BLEEDING
i. Brake fluid (recommended DOT 4)
ii. Brake bleeding kit (or a clean plastic hose)
iii. A small container to collect old brake fluid
iv. Wrenches
• Remove the screw to open reservoir cap (B).
• Remove the screws (A), reservoir cap (B),
diaphragm plate (C) and diaphragm (D).
NOTE
• Do not mix different types of fluid which are not compatible.
• Perform the bleeding procedure until the system is completely bleeded.
• For the last refill, fill the reservoir to the MAX level (be careful Not till the edge).
• Fill the reservoir with a new brake fluid from a
sealed container up to the edge.
• Remove the rubber cap (D) from the bleeder
screw of the caliper.
NOTES :- 165
2.5.1 FRONT DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
• Insert the bleeder transparent hose (E) after the
wrench.
• Loosen the bleeder screw and keep it open,
then press the front brake lever repeatedly until
all the air from brake system comes out. Tighten
the bleeder screw.
• Fill the reservoir with a new brake fluid from a
sealed container up to the edge. Press the front
brake lever repeatedly again until all the air
from the brake system comes out.
• Move the front brake lever to half stroke and
lock it in position using a cable tie.
• Loosen the bleeder screw and keep it open,
then push the caliper against the disc until the
pistons are completely inside the caliper.
NOTES :- 166
2.5.1 FRONT DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
• Press the front brake lever completely, close
the bleeder screw and repeatedly press the
front brake lever several times until the
pressure is recovered.
• Fill the master cylinder reservoir with DOT 4
brake fluid from a sealed container up to the
edge.
• Close the bleed valve and operate the brake
lever. If it still feels spongy, bleed the system
again. After tightening the system completely,
tighten the bleed valve to the specified torque.
Torque: 12 ~ 16 Nm.
• Wash the reservoir cap, plastic diaphragm and rubber membrane with water and
dry it completely with compressed air. Install the diaphragm, diaphragm plate
and reservoir cap.
• Tighten the screws of reservoir cap with torque of 0.8 ~ 1.5 Nm.
NOTES :- 167
2.5.2 REAR DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
REAR DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
• Remove the reservoir filler cap (A), plastic
diaphragm (B) and rubber membrane ( C ).
• Fill the reservoir with a new brake fluid from a
sealed container up to the edge.
• Remove the rubber cap (D) from the bleeder
screw of the caliper, insert the bleeder
transparent hose (E) after the wrench.
• Loosen the bleeder screw and keep it open,
then pump the rear brake pedal (F) several
times until all the air from the brake system
comes out.
NOTES :- 168
2.5.2 REAR DISC BRAKE BLEEDING
• Fill the reservoir with a new brake fluid from a
sealed container up to the edge.
• Perform the bleeding procedure until the
system is completely flushed/bleeded. Tighten
the bleeding screw. Torque: 12 ~ 16 Nm.
• Loosen the bleeder screw and keep it open,
then push the caliper against the disc until the
pistons are completely inside the caliper.
• Press the pedal, close the bleeder screw, and
apply the pedal several times until the pressure
is recovered.
• Refill fill the reservoir to the MAX level (be
careful Not till the edge)
• Wash the filler cap, plastic diaphragm and
rubber membrane with water and dry it
completely with compressed air. Close the
reservoir. Secure the bleeder screw with the
specified tightening torque, remove bleeder
hose and wrench, and clean the bleeder screw
with water and compressed air. Set the rubber
cap back to the bleeder screw.
NOTES :- 169
2.5.3 TROUBLESHOOTING
i. Spongy Brake Lever or Pedal:
a. Issue:
• If the brake lever or pedal feels spongy or has excessive travel, it indicates air in
the brake system.
b. Solution:
• Re-bleed the brake system to remove all the air bubbles. Follow the proper
bleeding procedure carefully.
• Check for leaks in the brake lines or caliper seals and repair or replace any
damaged components.
ii. No Fluid Flow from Bleeder Valve:
a. Issue:
• When you open the bleeder valve, no brake fluid flows out.
b. Solution:
• Check the master cylinder reservoir to ensure it is properly filled with brake fluid.
The reservoir should not run dry during the bleeding process.
• Make sure the bleeder valve is fully open.
• Verify that there are no blockages in the brake line or bleeder valve.
iii. Bleeder Valve Stripped or Damaged:
a. Issue:
• If the bleeder valve is stripped or damaged, it can be challenging to open or
close.
b. Solution:
• Carefully remove the damaged bleeder valve and replace it with a new one.
Ensure the replacement is of correct size and type.
• Use appropriate tools and take care not to over-tighten the bleeder valve when
installing it to prevent damages.
NOTES :- 117700
2.5.3 TROUBLESHOOTING
iv. Contaminated Brake Fluid:
a. Issue:
• If the brake fluid appears dirty or contaminated, it can affect braking
performance.
b. Solution:
• Perform a complete brake fluid flush by bleeding the entire system with a fresh
brake fluid.
• Ensure that you use the correct type of brake fluid as specified in your
motorcycle's owner's manual (usually DOT 4 for most motorcycles).
v. Inadequate Bleeding Sequence:
a. Issue:
• If you do not follow the correct bleeding sequence, air may remain trapped inthe
system.
b. Solution:
• Follow the sequence precisely to ensure that all calipers are bled properly.
vi. Leaking Brake Lines or Fittings:
a. Issue:
• Leaks in the brake lines or at the connections can introduce air into the system.
b. Solution:
• Inspect all brake lines and fittings for any signs of leaks. Tighten or replace any
leaking components.
NOTES :- 117711
2.5.4 BRAKE PADS
1. BRAKE PAD INSPECTION
2. CHECKING AND MONITORING OF PAD & DISC WEAR
NOTES :- 117722
2.5.4 BRAKE PADS
1. BRAKE PAD INSPECTION
(1) Wear Indicator Grooves (2) Brake Disc
• Brake pad wear depends upon the severity of usage, the type of riding, and road
conditions. Inspect the pads at each regular maintenance interval.
• Check the wear indicator grooves (1) in each pad.
• If either pad is worn to the bottom of the grooves, replace both pads as a set.
2. CHECKING AND MONITORING OF PAD & DISC WEAR
• The extend of brake pad wear can be checked
by observing the groove limit on the pad.
• When wear exceeds the groove limit, replace it
with new ones.
• Thickness should be more than 4.5 mm Always
replace the brake pad as a set, otherwise
braking performance will be adversely affected.
• If disc thickness is less than the above values,
change the disc. Also check disc for any
damages, cracks and deformation. If it shows
the above defects, change the disc.
• Check and clean brake pad at every service.
• Avoid the brake pad from any oil contact.
NOTES :- 117733

2.5.5 BRAKE CALIPER & PISTON
Removal of Piston & Seals from Caliper
• Remove the bellow & boot from the caliper
assembly.
• Hold the caliper body with the pistons facing
downwards.
• Blow low pressure compressed air with a nozzle
into the oil passage hole on the caliper to drive
out the pistons from the caliper bores.
• Gently remove the seals from caliper using a blunt and soft tool.
Assembly of Piston & Seals to Caliper
• Apply fresh oil to seals and piston while installing into caliper.
• Follow reverse removal procedure to install Piston and Seals.
NOTE
• If the pistons and seals are worn out or damaged, replace the entire caliper
assembly.
• When assembling the piston into the caliper, DO NOT apply force. Apply only a
small amount of pressure with your hand.

caliper, to drive out the piston from the caliper bore.

2.6 EXHAUST SYSTEM
1. General Information:
BSA vehicles are designed with a single exhaust on RH side. The Exhaust system has a
unique design and exhaust beat that carry the DNA of BSA motorcycles in it.
a. Features&Advantages:
• Stylish Upswept Exhaust.
• Aesthetics & Bassy Exhaust Note.
• Unique sound signature retains BSA DNA.

2. Muffler assembly
!! WARNING
Before dismantling any part of the exhaust system, allow the vehicle to cool down
completely. Working on the exhaust system when the vehicle is in extremely hot
condition may cause burns and life-threatening incidents to the operator.


222000 111000

NOTES :- 118844
2.6 EXHAUST SYSTEM
4. TROUBLESHOOTING
Troubleshooting the exhaust system in a BSA motorcycle is important for ensuring
proper performance and compliance with emissions regulations. Below mentioned is a
step-by- step guide to help you identify and address common exhaust system issues:
i. Listen for Unusual Sounds:
Start your motorcycle and listen for any abnormal noises coming from the
exhaust system. This could include hissing, popping or rattling sounds.
ii. Check for Leaks:
Leaks in the exhaust system can cause poor performance and noise. Look for
visible cracks, holes, or loose connections in the pipes, mufflers and joints.
iii. Inspect the Exhaust Gaskets:
Exhaust gaskets at the cylinder head and where the pipes connect to the
mufflers can deteriorate over time. Check for signs of wear or leaks at these
gasket locations.
iv. Examine the Exhaust Pipes:
Inspect the exhaust pipes for rust and corrosion. Surface rust can be normal,
but deep corrosion can weaken the pipes and lead to leaks.
v. Muffler Check:
Check the muffler for damages, rust, or holes. Make sure it is securely mounted
and has not become loose from its hangers.
vi. Examine the Header and Collector Pipes:
Inspect the header pipes that connect to the engine cylinders and the collector
pipe that joins the header pipes. Make sure they are properly connected and
undamaged.
vii.Check for Loose or Missing Fasteners:
Ensure that all nuts, bolts and clamps in the exhaust system are properly
tightened and in good condition. Loose fasteners can lead to leaks and rattling.
viii.Address Exhaust Smoke:
Excessive exhaust smoke (blue, white or black) can be a sign of engine-related
issues. If the smoke is not due to a simple cold start, it may require further
investigation to determine the cause.
NOTES :- 118855
2.6 EXHAUST SYSTEM
ix. Exhaust Odor:
If you notice a strong smell of exhaust fumes, it can be a sign of a leak or an
overly rich fuel-air mixture. Address this issue promptly to avoid health and safety
concerns.
x. Examine the Header Pip Assembly:
Inspect the header pipe assembly for rust and corrosion. Surface rust can be
normal, but deep corrosion can weaken the pipes and lead to leaks.
xi. Muffler Assembly Check:
Check the muffler for damages, rust, or holes. Make sure it is securely mounted
and has not become loose from its hangers.
xii.Examine the Header Pipes:
Inspect the header pipes that connect to the engine cylinders and make sure they
are properly connected and undamaged.
xiii.Check for Loose or Missing Fasteners:
Ensure that all nuts, bolts and clamps in the exhaust system are properly
tightened and in good condition. Loose fasteners can lead to leaks and rattling.
xiv.Address Exhaust Smoke:
Excessive exhaust smoke (blue, white or black) can be a sign of engine-related
issues. If the smoke is not due to a simple cold start, it may require further
investigation to determine the cause.
xv. Exhaust Odor:
If you notice a strong smell of exhaust fumes, it can be a sign of a leak or an
overly rich fuel-air mixture. Address this issue promptly to avoid health and
safety concerns.
NOTES :- 118866
2.7 COOLING SYSTEM
2.7.1 GENERAL GUIDELINES
2.7.2 RADIATOR
2.7.3 COOLANT FLUSHING
2.7.4 TROUBLESHOOTING
2.7.5 THERMOSTAT VALVE
NOTES :- 118877
2.7.1 GENERAL GUIDELINES
1. Introduction
When it comes to choosing the right coolant for your BSA motorcycle, like any
other vehicle, it is essential to follow the recommendations and guidelines.
However, we will provide you some general guidelines for using MOTUL INUGEL
EXPERT and CASTROL REDICOOL HD (PREMIX) coolants in your BSA motorcycle.
• Coolant Change Interval: Follow the recommended coolant change interval.
Regularly check the coolant level and condition in the reservoir.
• Flush the Cooling System: Before adding a new coolant, it is a good practice to
flush the cooling system to remove any old coolant, debris or contaminants.
Flushing should be done following the recommended instructions.
• Bleeding the Cooling System: After changing the coolant, ensure that you
properly bleed the cooling system to remove any air bubbles. Air pockets can
reduce cooling efficiency and lead to overheating.
• Maintenance Checks: Periodically inspect the cooling system components,
including hoses, radiator, and thermostat, for any signs of damage or wear.
Replace any damaged parts promptly.
• Coolant Level: Keep the coolant level within the recommended range. Avoid
overfilling, as excessive coolant can lead to system pressure issues.
• Avoid Mixing Coolants: It is generally not recommended to mix different types
or brands of coolant. Stick with one type and brand to maintain consistency and
effectiveness.
NOTE
Disposal of old Coolant: When disposing of old coolant, follow local environmental
regulations. Coolant is considered hazardous waste and should not be poured down
drains or onto the ground.
NOTES :- 118888

NOTES :- 119900
2.7.3 COOLANT FLUSHING
1. COOLANT FLUSHING
• Flushing a radiator basically means taking the radiator outside and giving it a
good clean by Flushing fresh water through it to get rid of any Junk.
• Refer Section 3.7 Cooling Refiling for coolant replacement process (Coolant
Flushing).
NOTE
a. Flushing radiator is strongly recommended for the following reasons:
• It removes scale deposits and rust.
• Scale deposits and rust build up in a radiator overtime.
• Flushing helps to take out these deposits, which get washed out with the
antifreeze.
b. BENEFITS OF RADIATOR FLUSH
• It removes all scale build-up and contaminants.
• It lubricates the water pump.
• It protects against future rust formation.
• It opens the opportunity to inspect the cooling system.
• Improve engine performance.
NOTES :- 119911
2.7.4 TROUBLESHOOTING
1. TROUBLESHOOTING
i. Symptom 1: Overheating
Solution: Overheating can result from various issues. Start by checking the coolant
level, radiator cap, and coolant mixture. Ensure the cooling system is properly bled
of air. Inspect the radiator for blockages, clean the fins, and check the fan's
operation. If the problem persists, it may be a faulty thermostat or radiator, which
should be inspected and replaced as needed.
ii. Symptom 2: Coolant Leak
Solution: A coolant leak can be caused by damaged hoses, loose connections, or a
faulty radiator. Inspect all hoses and connections for leaks or damage. Tighten
hose clamps as needed. If the radiator is leaking, it may require repair or
replacement.
iii. Symptom 3: Low Coolant Level
Solution: If the coolant level is consistently low, it's important to identify and fix the
source of the leak. Thoroughly inspect the radiator, hoses, water pump, and
connections for leaks. Replace any damaged components and top up the coolant
with the appropriate mixture.
iv. Symptom 4: Radiator Fan Not Working
Solution: A malfunctioning radiator fan can lead to overheating. Check the fan's
electrical connections and fuses. Test the fan motor by applying direct voltage to it.
If the fan still doesn't work, consider replacing the fan motor or the temperature
sensor responsible for fan activation.
v. Symptom 5: Coolant Dis-coloration or Contamination
Solution: If the coolant appears discolored or contaminated, it may be time for a
radiator flush and coolant replacement. Drain the old coolant, flush the radiator
with water, and refill it with the recommended coolant mixture according to BSA
specifications.
vi. Symptom 6: Persistent Air Bubbles in Cooling System
Solution: If you frequently notice air bubbles in the cooling system, it may indicate
an issue with the cooling system's sealing or bleeding. Check for any loose or
damaged gaskets, hoses, or connections. Properly bleed the cooling system to
remove air pockets.
vii.Symptom 7: Corroded or Damaged Radiator
Solution: If the radiator is visibly corroded or damaged, it may need to be replaced.
Consult your BSA motorcycle's manual for the recommended radiator replacement
procedure.
NOTES :- 119922
2.7.4 TROUBLESHOOTING
viii.Symptom 8: Rising Temperature During Idle
Solution: If the temperature rises significantly when the motorcycle is idling, it could
indicate a weak or failing water pump. Have the water pump inspected and replaced
if necessary.
ix. Symptom 9: Engine Misfiring or Poor Performance
Solution: In extreme cases, radiator issues can lead to engine overheating, causing
performance problems or even engine damage. If you experience engine misfires or
poor performance along with cooling system issues, it's crucial to address the cooling
problem promptly to prevent engine damage.
NOTE
Regular Interval Checkpoints:
A. Visual Inspection:
Start by visually inspecting the radiator for any obvious signs of damage, such as
leaks, cracks, or bent fins. Look for coolant stains or puddles under the bike,
which can indicate a leak.
B. Check Coolant Level:
Ensure that the coolant level in the radiator is at the appropriate level. Low
coolant levels can lead to overheating. Top it off with the correct coolant if
necessary.
C. Inspect Hoses and Connections:
Inspect the radiator hoses and their connections for any signs of damage, cracks,
or leaks. Tighten hose clamps as needed.
NOTES :- 119933
2.7.5 THERMOSTAT VALVE
1. GENERAL INFORMATION
2. LOCATION OF THERMOSTAT VALVE
3. DISMANTLING OF THERMOSTAT VALVE
4. ASSEMBLY OF THERMOSTAT VALVE
NOTES :- 119944
2.7.5 THERMOSTAT VALVE
1. General Information:
Thermostat valve is the important part of the cooling system and play a vital role in the
coolant circulation from engine to radiator.
The role of the thermostat is to control the flow of hot coolant through the radiator.
The thermostat is integrated and mounted in between the engine and radiator.
Function of Thermostat Valve:
• Removes excess heat from the engine.
• Maintains a constant engine temperature.
• Regulates the flow of coolant from engine to radiator.
2. Location of Thermostat Valve
Thermostat valve is located in between the engine and radiator. One end of the
thermostat is connected to the bypass hose (4) of the coolant. Where in the hot
coolant is enters the thermostat valve and the other end is connected to the hose
thermostat (6) from where the hot coolant enters the radiator for cooling.
3. Dismantling of Thermostat Valve
!! WARNING
Before dismantling any part of the cooling system. Allow the vehicle radiator to cool
completely and settle it down. Working on the cooling system when the coolant is
extremely hot condition may cause burns and life-threatening incidents to operator.
• Place the vehicle on a firm level ground / Ramp
using a paddock stand.
• Allow the vehicle radiator to cool down

completely. 3
• Drain the coolant lines completely before 1
dismantling thermostat valve.
• For draining the coolant from hoses / coolant 2
lines refer topic Coolant Flushing (2.7.3).


• Dislocate the spring band (2) clamps and clip (3)
from radiator-thermostat hose (1) using pliers. 4

• Remove the radiator-thermostat hose (1) from
the thermostat cover (4).
• Loosen and remove Screw Bolt-M6 (7) using No-10 T panner and take out
thermostat cover (4).
• Take out O-ring (5) and Thermostat from Cylinder head.
NOTES :- 119955
2.7.5 THERMOSTAT VALVE
4. Assembling of thermostat Valve Before assembling
Before assembling following point needs to be inspected:
• Check the functioning of the thermostat valve. If necessary, replace with the new
one.
• Clean the thermostat thoroughly with coolant or water. Ensure that are no burse
and dust particles accumulated in the valve body and spring.
• Check the hoses for any cuts/ damage. If required replace with the new one.
• Check the coolant leakage from coolant lines/ hoses.
• Check the tension of spring band clamps. If required replace with the new one.
• For assembling follow the reversal procedure of dismantling.
NOTE
• Check the coolant level after the assembly of thermostat valve. Top up the
coolant if required to the specified level. Coolant level must be maintained
between MIN & MAX level in the coolant recovery tank. Low coolant level may
cause engine heating issue and leads to engine seizure.
• After thermostat dismantling need to put coolant in radiator up to filler neck
area.

3.1.1 COMPRESSION PRESSURE CHECK
1. INTRODUCTION
2. PRESSURE CHECKING
3. TROUBLESHOOTING
NOTES :- 199
3.1.1 COMPRESSION PRESSURE CHECK
1. INTRODUCTION
Compression pressure is crucial in the BSA BANTAM motorcycle engine for several
reasons:
Engine Efficiency :
Compression pressure play vital role in the combustion process. It determine
show effectively the air-fuel mixture is compressed within the engine' s cylinders
before ignition. Proper compression ensures efficient combustion, which is essential
for generating power and torque.
Power Output :
Adequate compression pressure is necessary to produce the required power output.
Low compression can lead to reduced engine performance, resulting in sluggish
acceleration and reduced top speed.
Fuel Efficiency :
Engines with correct compression ratio stend to be more fuel-efficient. Proper
compression ensures that the air-fuel mixture is burned efficiently, which can lead to
better fuel economy.
Emissions:
A well-compressed air-fuel mixture burns more cleanly, leading to lower emissions of
harmful pollutants. This is important for both environmental reasons and to meet
emissions regulations.
Engine Longevity:
Maintaining the correct compression pressure is crucial for the longevity of the
engine. Low compression can be the cause of unburn air-fuel mixture. Maintaining
the working temperature of the engine is most important. it can increase wear and
tear, and potential damage to engine components.
Diagnostic Tool :
Compression pressure testing is a valuable diagnostic tool. It can help identify
various engine issues, such as worn piston rings, cylinder head gasket leaks, valve
problems, or cylinder wear. Detecting these issues early can prevent more significant
problems and costly repairs in the future.
Performance Assessment :
Periodically checking compression pressure allows you to as sess the overall health
of the engine. If the compression pressure is within the specified range, it indicates
that the engine is in good condition. If it falls outside of that range, it signal spot
initial problems that need to be addressed
NOTES :- 200
3.1.1 COMPRESSION PRESSURE CHECK
2. PRESSURE CHECKING
To check the compression pressure in a BSA motorcycle you'll need a compression
tester tool.
i. Gather necessary tools and materials
- Compression tester kit
- Wrenches and sockets
ii. Prepare the motorcycle:
- Ensure that the motorcycle is on a level surface and the engine is at normal
temperature.
- Remove the spark plug from the cylinder head.
iii. Use the Compression Tester:
- Screw the compression tester into the spark
plug hole of the cylinder. Hand-tighten it
securely.
iv. Disable Ignition
- Disable the ignition system to prevent the
engine from starting while testing. You can do
this by disconnecting the ignition coil, fuel
injector connector and fuel pump connector.
v. Crank the Engine:
- Crank the engine 4 to 5 time using the electric starter. This will build up
compression in the cylinder.
vi. Read the Gauge
- Observe the compression tester gauge. It should show the compression pressure
in PSI (pounds per square inch) or kPa
(kilopascals). Note the reading. Specification:
10-14 bar.
vii. Reassemble:
- After completing the compression test ,
reattach the spark plug and ignition
components if disconnected.
NOTES :- 201
3.1.1 COMPRESSION PRESSURE CHECK
3. TROUBLESHOOTING
i. Analyse the Results:
- If the compression pressure falls within the specified range for your motorcycle,
it indicates that the engine compression is healthy.
- Low Pressure suggests engine problems such as worn piston rings, cylinder walls,
or valves.
ii. Check Spark Plug Condition:
- Inspect the spark plugs. If the spark plug is fouled or damaged, it can affect
compression readings. Replace any worn or damaged spark plugs with the
correct type and gap.
iii. Wet Compression Test :
- If you suspect an issue with piston rings, you can perform a wet compression test.
This involves adding a small amount of oil (about a teaspoon) into the cylinder
through the spark plug hole and retesting the compression. If the compression
significantly improves after the wet test, it suggests that the issue may be related
to worn piston rings.
iv. Inspect Valves and Valve Seals:
- Compression problems can also be caused by issues with the valves or valve seals.
Check for proper valve clearance and make sure the valves are sealing correctly.
Replace worn valve seals if necessary.
v. Check for Leaks:
- Inspect the engine for any visible signs of oil or coolant leaks. Leaks in the
cylinder head gasket or other areas can result in reduced compression.
vi. Inspect Timing and Camshaft:
- Timing issues or a malfunctioning camshaft can affect compression. Ensure that
the timing marks are aligned correctly, and that the camshaft is in good
condition.
vii. Cylinder Bore and Piston Condition:
- If none of the above steps resolve the compression issue, it may be necessary to
inspect the cylinder bore and piston. If there is significant wear or damage to
these components, they may need to be replaced.
NOTES :- 202
3.1.1 COMPRESSION PRESSURE CHECK
NOTE
Remember that compression testing is just one aspect of diagnosing engine
problems. If you are experiencing performance issues or suspect engine problems,
it's best to practice performing a more comprehensive assessment and address any
issues that may be present.
NOTES :- 203
3.1.2 SPARK PLUG
1. INTRODUCTION
2. LOCATION
3. REMOVAL & ASSEMBLY
4. TROUBLESHOOTING
NOTES :- 204
3.1.2 SPARK PLUG
1. INTRODUCTION
Spark plug is a critical component in the ignition system of any internal
combustion engine.
Spark Plug
Here's an introduction to spark plug in BSA BANTAM motorcycles:
Purpose of Spark Plug:
Spark plug is essential for the ignition process in an internal combustion engine.
They create the spark needed to ignite the air-fuel mixture in the engine's
combustion chamber, initiating the power stroke that drives the motorcycle forward.
Spark Plug Location:
In BSA BANTAM motorcycles, the spark plug is positioned at the top of the engine's
cylinder head for easy access and maintenance.
Maintenance :
Regular sparks plug maintenance is essential for the smooth operation of the
motorcycle. Key maintenance practices include:
• Periodic inspection and cleaning of spark plug.
• Checking and adjusting the spark plug gap.
• Replacing worn or damaged spark plugs as needed.
• Ensuring proper torque when installing spark plug.
Gap Adjustment :
The spark plug gap—the distance between the center and ground electrode—must
be set according to manufacturer specifications. This gap directly affects:
• Spark intensity and ignition timing.
• Engine performance and fuel efficiency.
Proper gap adjustment ensures reliable ignition, smooth acceleration, and efficient
fuel combustion.
2. LOCATION IN VEHICLE
BSA BANTAM is designed with single spark plug positioned
at the top of the engine's cylinder head.
NOTES :- 205
3.1.2 SPARK PLUG
NOTE
• Remove the Seat and Fuel tank for Spark Plug
removal.
• Clean around the spark plug base with
compressed air before removing the spark plug
and be sure that no debris is allowed to enter
the combustion chamber.
3. REMOVAL & ASSEMBLY
• Initially, Remove the spark plug cap by hand.
• Use Special tool to remove spark plug After
loosening use spark plug suppressor cap to take
it out from cylinder head. Torque- 1.3 (1.1-1.5
Kgf-m).
• Ensure that spark plug grade is as per given
specifications i.e. Champion RG6HCC (FM)&
BOSCH.
• Clean the insulator tip and electrodes of the
plug, carefully using pointed scraper or spark
plug cleaner.
• Check the gap between the center and side
electrodes with a wire type feeler gauge.
• Champion RG6HCC (FM) - 0.6 - 0.7 mm BOSCH
- 0.7-0.8mm.
NOTES :- 206
3.1.2 SPARK PLUG
!! WARNING
Do not adjust the spark plug gap, if the gap is out of
specifications, Replace it with a new one.
• Refit the spark plug on the cylinder head.
• While replacing, hand tight the spark plug first
& tighten it with torque 1.3 (1.1-1.5) kgfm.
• Do not over tight the Spark plug.
• Ensure spark plug suppressor cap gets locked
properly.
4. TROUBLESHOOTING
If the spark plug is faulty, the motorcycle may exhibit the following symptoms:
• Engine misfires or hesitation during acceleration.
• Rough idling or poor overall engine performance.
• Reduced fuel efficiency, leading to increased fuel consumption.
• Difficulty starting the motorcycle, requiring multiple attempts.
Action :
Inspect Spark Plug Condition: Visually inspect the spark plug regularly to check for
signs of wear, fouling, or damage. Look for the following:
I. Fouling
If the spark plug electrodes are covered in black soot or oil deposits, it may
indicate a rich fuel mixture. Clean or replace fouled plugs as needed.
ii. Wear and Tear:
Check for signs of erosion or damage to the spark plug electrodes. A damaged
or excessively worn plug should be replaced.
NOTES :- 207
3.1.2 SPARK PLUG
iii. Gap:
Ensure that the spark plug gap (the distance between the center and ground
electrode) is within the manufacturer's specific range.
NOTE
Spark plug has a finite lifespan and should be replaced at the intervals specified in
BSA BANTAM maintenance schedule or the spark plug manufacturer's
recommendations. Even if they appear visually fine, they can degrade over time
and affect engine performance.
NOTES :- 208
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
1. GENERAL INFORMATION
2. SHIM SETTING PROCESS
3. VALVE CLEARANCE SETTING
4. REASSEMBLY PROCESS
5. TROUBLESHOOTING
NOTES :- 209
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
1. GENERAL INFORMATION
A. Introduction
A “shim setting" typically refers to the adjustment of the valve clearances using
shims or tappet shims. Valve clearances are the small gaps between the camshaft
lobes and the valve lifters (tappets) in the engine. It's important to maintain the
correct valve clearances to ensure the engine runs smoothly and efficiently.
Here's how it generally works:
• Valve Clearances: Over time, the valve clearances can change due to wear and
tear. This can result in poor engine performance and increased valve noise.
• Shims: Shims are thin, precisely machined pieces of metal that come in various
thicknesses. They are placed between the camshaft and the valve lifter (tappet)
to adjust the valve clearance.
• Adjustment: To adjust the valve clearance, measure the current clearance using
a feeler gauge.
• Replacement: If necessary, the shims may need to be replaced with new ones of
the appropriate thickness to achieve the correct valve clearance.
• Reassembly: Once the correct valve clearances are achieved, the engine
components are reassembled, and the motorcycle is ready to run smoothly.
NOTE
It is essential to follow the BSA BANTAM guidelines and specifications when adjusting
valve clearances. Incorrect valve clearance can lead to poor engine performance,
excessive valve wear, and other problems.
2. Shim setting process
STEP 1
• Remove the gauge inspection cover With the
help of a 4 mm Allen key.
NOTES :- 210
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
STEP 2
• Remove 5 bolts as shown in fig. Lift the cover-
up and remove the cylinder head cover with a
gasket. Allen size: -5 mm.
STEP 3
• Remove the slipper clutch cover 3 bolt.
Spanner size: 8 mm.
• Remove the slipper clutch gear with the washer.
STEP 4
• Remove the cap cover magneto 7 bolts.
spanner size: 8 mm.
• Remove the magneto cover with gasket.
Note: Always use new gasket during reinstallation.
NOTES :- 211
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
SETP 5
• Remove the inter mated gear along with the
washer.
STEP 6
• Use a 14 mm socket to rotate anticlockwise and
adjust the timing setting.
STEP 7
• Ensure the mark on both the cam sprocket &
cam lobe is aligned properly as shown in the
image.
• Ensure the TDC mark on the magneto cover &
magneto is also aligned.
• Match the punch mark of the rotor with the
projection of the crankcase to set engine valve
timing.
NOTES :- 212
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
STEP 8
• Insert the feeler gauge in between the
camshaft & valve lifter bucket as shown in the
picture & measure the clearance for all four
valves.
• Ensure all the valve clearance within the
specification. Feeler gauge
Specification - INTAKE SIDE 0.080 -0.110 mm &
EXHAUST SIDE 0.150 – 0.180 mm.
Feeler gauge
• If the clearances are not as per the 1
specification, record the measurements and
adjust them, accordingly, as shown in the
subsequent slides.
• Shim setting and valve clearance checking must
be performed when the engine is cold.
• Loosen the chain tensioner.
2 3
• Remove the upper chain guide and bolts.
• Remove all seven (7) bolts (spanner size: 8 mm)
and set the camshaft holder assembly aside.
NOTES :- 213
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
NOTE
Follow the camshaft holder bolt loosening
sequence as specified in the Camshaft
section.
• Remove the valve lifter bucket and measure the
thickness of the shims using a micrometer.
Record the measured dimensions.
• Replace the shim in each valve to adjust the
clearance within the specified range using a
feeler gauge for accurate measurement.
3. VALVE CLEARANCE SETTING:
• Follow the same procedure for all valves.
• Position the camshaft and verify the TDC mark alignment on the sprocket (as
shown in the earlier slide).
• Install the camshaft holder and tighten all bolts to 11 Nm (range: 9-12 Nm)
torque as per Torque chart.
• Assemble the chain tensioner and recheck the TDC mark alignment.
• Measure the valve clearance again.
• If the valve clearance is not within the specified range, repeat the process until
the correct clearance is achieved.
4. REASSEMBLY PROCESS
• After tightening the camshaft holder to 11 Nm (range: 9-12 Nm) torque, install
the upper chain guide and its bolt.
• Place the cylinder head top cover, ensuring it is properly fitted on the cylinder
head cover rubber gasket.
• Clean the rubber gasket sealing surface and apply the specified sealant to the
rubber gasket.
• Tighten the cylinder head cover special bolts to 11 Nm (range: 9-12 Nm) torque.
• Tighten the tensioner screw to 5 Nm (range: 4-6 Nm) torque.
• Reassemble all vehicle parts and refer to the vehicle torque table for correct bolt
tightening specifications.
• Start the vehicle and check for any abnormal noise or leakages from the engine.
NOTES :- 214
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
NOTE
• If the clearances are not within the specified range, record the measurements
and adjust them accordingly as shown in the subsequent slides.
• Shim setting and valve clearance checking must be performed when the engine is
cold to ensure accurate measurements.
5. TROUBLESHOOTING
Troubleshooting during shim setting, specifically for adjusting valve clearances in a
motorcycle engine, is essential to ensure the process is performed correctly.
Below are some common issues and troubleshooting steps for shim setting:
i. Inaccurate Valve Clearance Measurement:
a. Issue: If the initial valve clearance measurement is inaccurate, incorrect
adjustments may follow.
b. Troubleshooting: Double-check the feeler gauge measurements for accuracy.
Ensure the engine is in the correct position before taking measurements.
ii. Incorrect Shims Installed:
a. Issue: Using the wrong thickness of shims can result in incorrect valve clearances.
b. Troubleshooting: Confirm that the correct shims are being used for the
motorcycle's engine. Measure the shim thickness with a micrometer before
installation.
iii. Difficulty in Removing or Installing Shims:
a. Issue: Shims can sometimes be difficult to remove or install due to tight
clearances or other factors.
b. Troubleshooting: Lubricate the shims and tappet surfaces with engine oil to
ease installation and removal. Use a magnetic tool to handle and position shims if
available.
iv. Valve Noise or Poor Engine Performance After Adjustment:
a. Issue: If the valve clearances are still incorrect after adjustment, valve noise or
poor engine performance may occur.
b. Troubleshooting: Recheck the valve clearances to ensure they are within the
BSA BANTAM specifications. Ensure all components are properly reassembled
and torqued to the correct values.
NOTES :- 215
3.1.3 SHIM SETTING
NOTE
Further inspection of the valve train, camshaft, or tappets may be needed if issues
persist.
v. Uneven Valve Clearances:
a. Issue: Valve clearances on one side of the engine are different from the other.
b. Troubleshooting: Check for discrepancies in the camshaft or tappet installation.
Ensure you are adjusting the correct valves, as some engines have different
intake and exhaust valve clearances. Verify that the engine is at the Top Dead
Centre (TDC) for the cylinder you are working on.
vi. Damaged Shims or Components:
a. Issue: Shims or other engine components may become damaged during the
adjustment process.
b. Troubleshooting: Inspect shims and related components for any signs of
damage. Replace any damaged shims or parts as necessary.
vii. Valve Clearances Drifting Out of Spec Over Time:
a. Issue: Valve clearances may change after a period of use.
b. Troubleshooting: Regularly monitor and maintain valve clearances as part of
routine maintenance. If clearances change rapidly, there may be underlying
issues with the valve train, such as worn cam lobes, tappets, or valve seats. A
more detailed inspection may be necessary in such cases.
!! WARNING
Shim setting is a critical engine adjustment, and precision is essential to ensure the
engine runs smoothly and efficiently.
NOTES :- 216
3.1.4 OIL DRAINING & OIL FILTER REPLACEMENT
1. GENERAL GUIDELINES
2. ENGINE OIL LEVEL
3. ENGINE OIL REPLACEMENT
4. ENGINE OIL FILTER REPLACEMENT
5. TROUBLESHOOTING
NOTES :- 217
3.1.4 OIL DRAINING & OIL FILTER REPLACEMENT
1. General Guidelines:
• Viscosity Rating (SAE Grade) :
The first thing to consider is the viscosity of the oil, typically represented by the SAE
(Society of Automotive Engineers) grade. The "W" stands for winter, and it indicates
the oil's flow characteristics at colder temperatures. The larger the W number, the
better the oil flows in cold weather.
The second number represents the oil's viscosity at operating temperature. Higher
numbers indicate thicker oil, which is better for high-temperature conditions.
• Oil Type & Specifications:
i. MOTUL H-TECH 100 4T 10W 50 / CASTROL – 10W50 JASO MA-2
ii. SAE 10W50 fully synthetic motorcycle engine oil that meets JASO - MA2 and API-
SL (or Higher) specification.
• Oil Capacity:
Be sure to use the correct oil capacity specified in your owner's manual.
Overfilling or under filling can lead to engine problems.
• Oil Change Interval:
Follow the recommended oil change interval in your owner's & Workshop manual.
• Oil Filter:
Make sure to change it at the same time you change the oil. Refer to the
BSA BANTAM part Catalogue for the correct filter type.
• Environmental Considerations:
Dispose of used oil responsibly by taking it to a recycling center or an auto parts
store that accepts used oil. Do not dump used oil into the environment.
Tools and Materials Needed :
• Oil filter (if applicable) & Oil Drain pan
• Funnel
• Paper Towel
• Paddock stand
• Appropriate size wrenches or socket set
NOTES :- 218
3.1.4 OIL DRAINING & OIL FILTER REPLACEMENT
2. Engine Oil Level
i. Engine Oil Level Inspection

SL (or Higher) specification.
iii. Engine Oil Capacity and Service Intervals:

Engine oil quality significantly impacts engine service life. Follow the maintenance
schedule for oil changes and replace oil more frequently in dusty conditions.
Changing the oil filter requires a special tool.

3.1.4 OIL DRAINING & OIL FILTER REPLACEMENT
5. Troubleshooting
a) Low Oil Level:
Symptoms:
Low oil pressure warning light, engine knocking or tapping noises, poor
engine performance.
Troubleshooting:
Check the oil level through clutch cover window sight glass. If it is low, top up with
the recommended oil type and quantity as specified in the owner's manual.
b) Oil Leaks:
Symptoms:
Visible oil puddles under the motorcycle, oil-soaked parts or components,
low oil level.
Troubleshooting:
Identify the source of the leak, which can be from gaskets, seals, or loose fittings.
Tighten or replace the faulty component. It is important to fix oil leaks promptly to
prevent oil loss and potential engine damage.
c) Excessive Oil Consumption:
Symptoms:
Frequent need to top up oil, excessive smoke from the exhaust, fouled spark plugs.
Troubleshooting:
Check for oil leaks, as mentioned above. If there are no visible leaks, the engine may
be burning oil. This could be due to worn piston rings or valve seals. Consult a
mechanic for a comprehensive inspection and repair.
d) Oil Contamination:
Symptoms:
Dis-colored or milky oil, coolant loss, overheating, or poor engine performance.
Troubleshooting:
If you notice coolant mixing with the oil (resulting in a milky appearance), it could
indicate a blown head gasket or cracked engine block. Address these issues promptly
to prevent further damage.
NOTES :- 222
3.1.4 OIL DRAINING & OIL FILTER REPLACEMENT
e) Oil Pressure Problems:
Symptoms:
Low oil pressure warning light, abnormal engine noises, poor performance.
Troubleshooting:
Use a mechanical oil pressure gauge to measure the actual oil pressure. If it is below
the specified range, this could indicate a worn oil pump, clogged oil passages, or low
oil level.
f) Oil Viscosity Issues:
Symptoms:
Difficulty starting the engine in cold weather, excessive engine noise,
reduced fuel efficiency.
Troubleshooting:
Ensure you are using the correct oil viscosity grade recommended for your
motorcycle. Changing to the appropriate oil grade can often resolve these
issues.
g) Dirty or Contaminated Oil:
Symptoms:
Reduced engine performance, increased engine wear, poor fuel efficiency.
Troubleshooting:
Regularly change the engine oil and filter at the recommended intervals. Ensure that
you use the recommended oil and filters. Contaminated oil can cause serious
damage, so it is crucial to maintain clean oil.
h) Oil Foaming:
Symptoms:
Increased engine noise, poor lubrication, overheating.
Troubleshooting:
Foaming can occur due to improper oil levels or aeration of the oil. Check the oil level
and ensure it is within the recommended range. If the problem persists, inspect the
oil pump for any issues.
NOTES :- 223
33..12. 4E NOGILI NDER RAEINMINOGV A&L O&I LI NFSILTTAELRL ARTEIPOLNA CFREOMMEN FTRAME

33..12. 4E NOGILI NDER RAEINMINOGV A&L O&I LI NFSILTTAELRL ARTEIPOLNA CFREOMMEN FTRAME
1. Service Information:
I. GENERAL
• When removing/installing the engine, tape the frame around the engine
beforehand for frame protection.
• When installing the engine, be sure to tighten the engine mounting fasteners to
the specified torque in the specified sequence. If you mistake the torque or
sequence, loosen all mounting fasteners, then tighten them again to the
specified torque in the correct sequence.
The following components can be serviced with the engine installed in the
frame.
- Starter motor
- Throttle body
- Oil pump
- Camshaft
- Clutch
- Flywheel - Magneto
- Stator
- Cylinder head/valves
- Piston/cylinder block
The following components require engine removal for service.
- Transmission & Gear Shifting
- Crankshaft/Balancer
Tools and Materials Needed:
• Motorcycle stands
• Paddock stand
• Appropriate size wrenches or socket set
• Screwdriver
• Allen keys
• Block of wood (for supporting the engine, if needed)
NOTES :- 225
33..12. 4E NOGILI NDER RAEINMINOGV A&L O&I LI NFSILTTAELRL ARTEIPOLNA CFREOMMEN FTRAME
2. Engine Removal from Vehicle
• Disconnect the Battery:
Disconnect the motorcycle's battery to prevent any
electrical hazards during the engine removal
process.
• Drain Fluids:
Drain the engine oil and, if applicable, the coolant
from the motorcycle. Make sure to properly dispose
of these fluids in accordance with local regulations.
• Remove Cylinder head pipe:
Remove any hoses that the cylinder head
connected to the radiator tank.
• Remove radiator pipe:
• Remove any hoses that connect to the radiator
tank. These hoses are secured with hose clamps.
• Use pliers to loosen and remove hose clamps.
NOTES :- 226
33..12. 4E NOGILI NDER RAEINMINOGV A&L O&I LI NFSILTTAELRL ARTEIPOLNA CFREOMMEN FTRAME
• Front Sprocket :
• Remove the front sprocket bolt and with
washer-lock plate with the drive chain.
• Disconnect Wiring and Cables:
• Label and disconnect all electrical connections
and cables that are attached to the engine,
including the spark plug wires, ignition system,
and any sensors.
• Remove Fuel System:
• Turn off the fuel supply and disconnect the fuel
lines.
• Disconnect the throttle body from the engine.
For our engine both side washers were added
and an M8 bolt.
• Support the Engine:
Before lifting the engine out, ensure it is properly
supported. You can use an engine hoist or lift to
support the engine's weight. Attach suitable straps
or chains to the engine to lift it evenly.
• Locate all Engine Mountings:
• Locate and remove all the mounting bolts that
secure the engine to the frame. Refer to the
images for specific locations.

• Exhaust System
• Remove header pipe connection from engine
and completely remove header pipe assembly.
NOTE
• It is essential to support the motorcycle on a lift or jack to ensure stability during
this process.
• Lift the Engine:
• Carefully lift the engine out of the frame using the engine hoist. Make sure the
engine clears all surrounding components and the frame without causing any
damage.

3. Engine Reinstallation to Vehicle
• To reinstall the engine, reverse the steps above, ensuring that all bolts are tightened
as per specifications.
• Fill the engine with oil and coolant.
4. Troubleshooting
i. Start with Basic Inspection:
• Ensure there is enough fuel in the tank.
• Check the oil level and quality. Old or contaminated oil can affect engine
performance.
ii. Check for Fuel Issues:
• Inspect the fuel lines for leaks or blockages.
• Clean or replace the fuel filter.
• Check the throttle body for clogs, and ensure the float level is correct.
• Verify that the choke is functioning correctly.
NOTES :- 229
33..12. 4E NOGILI NDER RAEINMINOGV A&L O&I LI NFSILTTAELRL ARTEIPOLNA CFREOMMEN FTRAME
iii. Ignition System:
• Check the spark plug wires for damage or loose connections.
• Examine the spark plugs for fouling or wear, and replace if necessary.
• Verify the timing is set correctly according to your vehicle's specifications.
iv. Air intake and Exhaust
• Make sure the air filter is clean and not clogged. Check the exhaust system for
leaks or obstructions that could affect engine performance.
v. Compression:
• Conduct a compression test to ensure the engine is building the correct level of
compression. Low compression can indicate worn piston rings or cylinder
damage.
vi. Valve Adjustment:
• Check and adjust the valve clearance to ensure proper valve operation.
vii. Electrical System:
• Inspect the battery for a proper charge and clean, tight connections. Check the
wiring harness for damaged or corroded wires. Ensure the charging system
(Alternator) is working correctly.
viii.Fuel System:
• Clean and rebuild the Throttle body if needed.
• Check the fuel pump pressure or any other fault.
ix. Exhaust Smoke Analysis:
• Black smoke: Indicates a rich fuel mixture.
• White smoke: Indicates coolant entering the combustion chamber, potentially
due to a blown head gasket.
• Blue smoke: Indicates burning oil, which could be due to worn piston rings or
valve seals.

NOTES :- 231
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
1. SERVICE INFORMATION
General
• This section covers the service of the valves, and camshaft. These services can be
done with the engine installed in the frame.
• When disassembling, mark and store the disassembled parts to ensure that they
are reinstalled in their original locations.
• Clean all disassembled parts with cleaning solvent and dry them by blowing them
off with compressed air before inspection.
• Valve and camshaft lubricating oil is fed through oil passage in the cylinder head.
Clean the oil passages before assembling cylinder head.
• Be careful not to damage the mating surfaces when removing the cylinder head
cover and cylinder head.
2. TROUBLESHOOTING
• Engine top-end problems usually affect engine performance. These can be
diagnosed by a compression test, or by tracing noises to the top-end with a
sounding rod or stethoscope.
• If the performance is poor at low speeds, check for a white smoke in the
crankcase breather hose. If the hose is smoky, check for seized piston ring.
Compression too low, hard starting or poor performance at low speed.
• Valves:
- Incorrect valve clearance
- Burned or bent valve
- Incorrect valve timing
- Broken valve spring
- Uneven valve seating
- Valve stuck open
• Cylinder head:
- Leaking or damaged cylinder head gasket.
- Loose spark plug.
- Warped or cracked cylinder head.
NOTES :- 232
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Cylinder/piston problem
- Compression too high, overheating or knocking.
- Excessive carbon build-up on the piston head or combustion chamber.
Excessive smoke
• Worn valve stem or valve guide
• Damaged stem seal
• Cylinder/piston problem
Excessive noise
• Incorrect valve clearance
• Sticking valve or broken valve spring
• Excessively worn valve seat
• Worn or damaged camshaft
• Worn shaft
Worn valve stem end
• Loose or worn cam chain
• Worn or damaged cam chain tensioner
• Worn cam sprocket teeth
• Cylinder / piston problem
Rough idle
• Low cylinder compression

3. CYLINDER HEAD DISMANTLING
• Stop the engine, disconnect the spark plug
caps, and remove the spark plugs.
• Disconnect the fuel pump unit.
• After the engine has cooled, remove the
cylinder head cover assembly as shown. Bolt
Step : 5 Nos. – Allen size :- 5mm.
• Remove the slipper clutch cover 3 bolts
Spanner size : 8 mm.
• Remove the slipper clutch Gear with both
washers by hand.
• Remove the cap cover magneto bolts. Remove
the bolts in crises- cross direction. Also remove
stator assembly.

• Magneto Cover:
M6 x 60mm = 3 Nos.
M6 x 70 mm = 4 Nos.
Socket Spanner size: 8mm.
• Stator Bolts & Screw:

1. M5 x 12 Screw= 1 Nos.

2. M5x10 Bolt= 2 Nos.
3. M5 x 30 Bolt= 3 Nos. 3
Socket Spanner or Allen Key size: 4mm.
+ Screw Driver

• Remove the Cylinder block to head inner CAP
NUT from RH side.
Head Cap Nut- 2 Nos.- M10.
Socket Spanner- 15 mm.
• Carefully loosen and remove all cylinder head
bolt.
Hex Bolt : 2 No. – M6 x 130 mm.
Socket / T- Spanner size : 8 MM.
NOTES :- 243
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Gently lift the cylinder head sub-assembly. If
necessary, use a rubber mallet to break the seal-
do not pry with a screwdriver to avoid
damaging the mating surfaces.
• Place the removed cylinder head on a clean, flat
surface to prevent contamination or damage.
!! CAUTION
“Handle the cylinder head with care during
maintenance. Avoid tapping excessively and ensure
not to damage the mating surface with tools such
as screwdrivers.”
• Carefully remove each tappet from its position.
Keep them organized to ensure they are
reinstalled in their original locations.
• Gently take out the shim from beneath the lifter
bucket. Avoid scratching or damaging any
components.
NOTES :- 244
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
NOTE
Store the shims and lifter buckets in a labeled tray to maintain proper order for
reassembly.
• Record the measured shim size for reference
during reassembly or adjustment.
• If the shim shows signs of excessive wear,
replace it with a new one of the correct
thickness.
• Carefully extract the valve cotter using special
tool.
NOTE
To prevent loss of tension, do not compress the valve springs more than
necessary. Excessive compression may weaken the spring, affecting engine
performance and reliability. Always use the appropriate tool and apply
controlled pressure.
• Remove the valve retainer and spring by hand.
• Once the valve retainer and spring have been
removed, carefully slide the valve out from the
cylinder head by hand.
NOTES :- 245
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Measure the existing shim thickness using a
Vernier caliper. (Example: 2.60 mm).
4. CYLINDER HEAD INSTALLATION
• Set the Engine to TDC:
• Rotate the crankshaft (via the Magneto
Assembly) until the Top Dead Center (TDC)
marking aligns.
• Use an 8 mm Allen key to rotate the crankshaft
if necessary.
• Verify Rotor Alignment:
• Through the inspection window, ensure the
rotor marking is properly aligned with the TDC
reference mark.
• Double-check that the piston is at TDC on the
compression stroke before proceeding with
cylinder head installation.
• Installing the Cylinder Head Sub-Assembly
1. Prepare the Components:
• Ensure all mating surfaces are clean and free
from debris.
• Wipe off any oil from the journals of the
cylinder head, camshaft, and camshaft holder
using a lint-free cloth.
• Install the valve from combustion chamber side
of the cylinder head.
NOTES :- 246
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Install the inner and Outer valve springs.
• Install the upper valve spring retainer and
prepare Valve Pressing tool to apply pressure to
install the valve cotter.
• Apply pressure using special tool and carefully
place valve retainer.
• Release the pressure slowly after installing
valve cotter.
• Place the shim (as measured earlier while
disassembly) on top of spring retainer.
NOTES :- 247
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Install the tappet by hand and check for proper
fitment.
• Repeat same process to install all remaining
valves.
• Inspection of Cylinder Head Component
Before reassembly, thoroughly inspect the following parts for damage,
abnormal wear, deformation, burning, or clogs in oil passages:
1. Cylinder Head – Check for cracks, warping, and clogged oil passages. Use a
straightedge and feeler gauge to measure warpage.
2. Valve Springs – Inspect for cracks, uneven wear, or loss of tension. Measure free
length and squareness against specifications.
3. Valves – Look for burnt edges, bending, or excessive wear on the valve face and
stem. Ensure the valve seats properly in the cylinder head.
4. Valve Guides – Check for wear by inserting the valve stem and measuring lateral
movement. Replace if clearance exceeds the limit.
5. Dowel Pins – Ensure they are not bent, worn, or damaged, as they help in proper
alignment of the cylinder head.
6. Oil Passages – Use compressed air to clear any blockages and ensure proper oil
flow.
Action: Replace any component that exceeds the service limit as per the
manufacturer’s specifications.
!! CAUTION
Ensure to use a new Cylinder Head Gasket when assembling the Cylinder Head.
Reusing an old gasket may lead to improper sealing, resulting in loss of
compression, oil leaks, or coolant leaks. Always verify the correct orientation
and alignment of the gasket before securing the
cylinder head.
• Apply a thin coat of thread-locking adhesive
(Loctite 243) to the threads of nuts and studs to
prevent loosening due to vibrations.
NOTES :- 248
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Install outer and inner side Cylinder block to
Head CAP NUT.
Head Cap Nut- 4 Nos.- M10
Socket Spanner- 15 mm .
Torque- 5.3 kgfm.
• Apply a thin coat of thread-locking adhesive
(Loctite 243) to the threads of bolt before
fixing.
• Fit the head bolts from intake side to the
Cylinder block
Hex Bolt : 2 No. – M6 x 130 mm
Torque- 1.1 Kgfm
• Before installation, remove any carbon deposits
from the combustion chamber to ensure a proper seal and efficient combustion.
Inserting the Dowel Pin & Refitting Inlet/ Exhaust Camshafts
1. Inspect the Dowel Pin:
• Check for damage, abnormal wear,
deformation, or burning.
• If the dowel pin is worn beyond the service
limit, replace it with a new one.
2. Insert the Dowel Pin:
• Carefully position the dowel pin into its
designated slot on the cylinder head.
• Ensure it is properly seated for accurate
alignment.
3. Refit Inlet and exhaust Camshafts:
• Carefully position the timing chain guide into its
designated slot in the cylinder block.
• Ensure it aligns properly with the timing chain
path to avoid misalignment or chain slack.
NOTES :- 249
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• fully position the exhaust camshaft in its correct
orientation within the cylinder head.
• Align the camshaft timing marks with the timing
chain to ensure correct valve timing.
• Refit the Intake Camshaft assembly. Inspect the
camshaft sprockets for damaged and worn
teeth. Replace if necessary.
• Evenly coat all camshaft lobes with a thin layer
of grease to ensure smooth contact with the
valve lifters .
• Also apply grease to the camshaft journals for
proper rotation and reduced friction.
• Fit the upper camshaft holder along with its
bolts. Flanged Hex Bolt : 7 Nos. - M6 x 35 mm
Socket Spanner size : 8 mm Tighten until
finger tight, and then by socket-spanner in
crisscross direction Torque: 1.1 Kgfm.
!! CAUTION
Never fit a camshaft sprocket to a camshaft using incorrectly identified bolt holes.
Severe engine damage will result from incorrect attachment.
NOTES :- 250
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Ensure marking on both the cam sprockets are TDC TDC
aligned properly are as shown in the MARK MARK
photograph. IN EX
• Apply a thin coat of thread-locking adhesive
(Loctite 243) to the threads of bolts before
fixing.
• Fit the chain tensioner. Apply a thin coat of
thread-locking adhesive (Loctite 243) to the
threads of bolt before fixing. Flange Bolt : 2 No.
– M6x22 mm Socket Spanner size : 8 mm.
• Tighten until finger tight, and then by socket
spanner. Torque: 1.1 Kgfm.
!! CAUTION
• Never fit the camshaft sprockets without
correctly setting the camshaft timing using the
service tools and timing procedure. Incorrect
valve timing adjustment can cause severe engine
damage, including piston-to-valve contact and
poor engine performance.
• Checking Valve Clearance (Engine Cold
1. Ensure the Engine is Cold
• Valve clearance measurements should be taken
when the engine iscompletely cold to ensure accuracy.
NOTES :- 251
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
2. Position the Camshaft
• Rotate the crankshaft to align the camshaft lobes away from the valve lifters.
• (TDC compression stroke for the cylinder being measured).
3. Measure Valve Clearance
• Insert a filler gauge between the camshaft lobe and the valve lifter (shim).
• Use the appropriate gauge for each valve type.
• The correct size gauge should slide in with slight resistance.
4. Adjust if Necessary
• If the clearance is out of specification, replace or adjust the shim size accordingly.
• Recheck the clearance after adjusting.
5. Inspect the Valves
• Check for damage, abnormal wear, deformation, or burning.
• Replace any valve that exceeds the service limit.
!! CAUTION
Always ensure valve clearance is within the specified range and adjust as
necessary to maintain optimal engine performance and longevity.
Failure to check and correct valve clearances can lead to severe engine issues.
Excessive wear may cause the valves to remain partly open, resulting in:
- Loss of engine performance due to improper combustion.
- Burnt valves and valve seats, leading to costly repairs.
- Serious engine damage from improper sealing, overheating, or misfiring.
• Use a 14 mm T-spanner to rotate the crankshaft
and ensure the rotor marking is properly
aligned with the TDC reference mark.
NOTES :- 252
3.3 CYLINDER HEAD / VALVE / CAMSHAFT
• Install LH Magneto cover and starter cover.
• Tighten all bolts to specified torque- 1.1 kgfm.
• Ensure the cylinder head cover gasket is
properly seated.
• Carefully place the cylinder head cover onto the
cylinder head.
• Fit the bolts to the cylinder head cover. Apply a
thin coat of thread-locking adhesive (Loctite
243) to the threads of bolt before fixing. Sleeve
Bolt: 5 Nos.
• Allen 5mm Tighten until finger tight, and then
by socket-spanner. Torque : 1.1 Kgfm

3.4 BLOCK PISTON ASSEMBLY
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
3.4.2 PISTON AND RINGS
NOTES :- 254
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
1. SERVICE INFORMATION
2. TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS
NOTES :- 255
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
1. SERVICE INFORMATION
General
• The crankcase must be separated to service the crankshaft assembly and pistons
and connecting rods.
• Mark and store the connecting rods, bearing caps, and bearing inserts to be sure
of their correct locations for reassembly.
• The crankpin and connecting rod bearing inserts are select fit and are identified
by color codes. Select replacement bearings from the code tables. After selecting
new bearings, recheck the oil clearance & axial play with gauge. Incorrect
clearances can cause major engine damage.
• Clean the oil passages in the upper crankcase with compressed air before
installing the pistons.
2. TROUBLESHOOTING
• Engine top-end problems usually affect engine performance. These can be
diagnosed by a compression test, or by tracing noises to the top-end with a
sounding rod or stethoscope.
• If the performance is poor at low speeds, check for a white smoke in the
crankcase breather hose. If the hose is smoky, check for seized piston ring.
Cylinder compression is too low, hard to starting, or poor performance at low
speed
• Leaking cylinder head gasket:
• Loose spark plug:
• Worn, stuck or broken piston ring
• Worn or damaged cylinder and piston
Compression too high, overheating or knocking
• Excessive carbon built-up on piston head or combustion chamber.
Excessive smoke
• Worn cylinder, piston, or piston ring
• Improper installation of piston rings
• Scored or scratched piston or cylinder wall
NOTES :- 256
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
Abnormal noise
• Worn piston pin or piston pin hole
• Worn connecting rod small end
• Bent connecting rod
• Worn cylinder, piston, or piston rings
• Worn main journal bearings
• Worn crankpin bearings
Engine vibration
• Excessive crankshaft runout

• LIST OF SPECIAL TOOLS
SR.NO DESCRIPTION TOOLS PART NO.

NOTES :- 262
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
CYLINDER BLOCK DISMANTLING
• Carefully disconnect the cylinder pipe hose
from its fittings.
• Check for cracks, wear, or leaks. Replace if
necessary.
• Gently pull the cylinder black pipe out by hand.
• Avoid using excessive force to prevent damage.
• Carefully lift and remove the cylinder head
gasket from the mating surface.
• Inspect for wear, cracks, or deformation and
replace with a new gasket.
• Clean any residual gasket material from the
cylinder block and head surfaces.
• Gently peel off the cylinder head gasket from
the cylinder block.
• Avoid using sharp tools that could damage the
mating surface.
NOTES :- 263
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Carefully lift the cylinder block upwards while
ensuring the piston remains in place.
• If the block is stuck, gently tap it with a rubber
mallet to loosen it.
• Once removed, take off the cylinder block
gasket and discard it.
• Inspect the gasket surface for any residue or
damage and clean it before reassembly.
• Remove the Piston Circlip by using special tool.
• Hold the circlip securely to prevent it From
flying off.
• Do not reuse old circlips- always
replace them with new ones.
• Remove the Piston Pin by using
special tool.
• Hold the piston securely to prevent
unnecessary movement while removing the pin.
• Ensure the connecting rod is stable and does
not move excessively.
• Insert the piston pin removal tool into the
piston pin bore.
• Apply steady and even pressure to push the
piston pin out of the piston and connecting rod.
• Avoid using excessive force to prevent damaging the piston or small end of the
connecting rod.
• Remove the Piston assembly
• After removing the piston circlip and piston pin,
carefully slide the piston assembly off the
connecting rod.
• Handle the piston carefully to avoid scratching
or damaging its surface.
NOTES :- 264
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Apply steady pressure & Remove the poison pin.
• Magneto Side Disassembly
• Remove the Slipper clutch Gear with washer:
• Gently take out the slipper clutch gear along
with its washer.
• Keep the washer safely to avoid misplacement.
• Remove the Magneto Cover Bolts:
• Loosen and remove the Step Bolts in a
crisscross pattern to prevent uneven pressure.
Use an 8 mm socket spanner for removal.
• Bolt Details:
• M6 x 60mm = 3 Nos.
• M6 x 70 mm = 4 Nos.
NOTES :- 265
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Remove the Magneto Cover with Gasket
• Loosen and Remove the Magneto Cover.
• After removing the magneto cover bolts,
carefully pull off the magneto cover.
• If the cover is stuck, gently tap it with a rubber
mallet to loosen it.
• Do not pry with a metal tool, as this can damage
the mating surfaces.
• Carefully peel off the magneto cover gasket
from the surface.
• Inspect for any tears, cracks, or damage, replace
the gasket if necessary.
• Carefully lift out the inter-mated gear along
with its washer.
• Ensure the washer is kept safe to prevent
misplacement.
• Use a Magneto Holder Tool to securely hold the
magneto rotor in place.
NOTES :- 266
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Using a 14 mm socket spanner and a torque
wrench, loosen the magneto nut (Hex Bolt:
M10).
• Take out the Washer by hand & keep it in secure
place.
• Attach the rotor puller tool to the rotor.
• Apply even pressure to carefully extract the
rotor.
• Once released, carefully remove the rotor
assembly by hand.
NOTES :- 267
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Take out the One way clutch starter gear with
its washer and needle roller bearing.
• Carefully disconnect and remove the neutral
switch from the engine casing.
• Clutch Cover Side Disassembly
• Remove the RH clutch cover assembly with
following bolts using 8mm socket spanner:
M6x40 - 4
M6x50 - 5
M6x65 - 2
NOTES :- 268
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Secure the primary gear holder tool to hold the
clutch assembly in place.
• This prevents rotation while loosening the
bolts.
• Using a 10 mm socket spanner, gradually loosen
the 3 Hex Bolts (M6 x 25 mm) simultaneously to
avoid uneven pressure.
• Carefully remove the clutch spring and set it
aside.
• Carefully slide out the clutch plates one by one.
NOTES :- 269
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Keep them in order of removal to ensure
correct reassembly.
• Take out the clutch plates and clutch shaft.
• After removing the plates, carefully pull out the
clutch shaft.
• Check for scoring, wear, or damage on the shaft
surface.
• Clean and lubricate the shaft before
reinstallation.
• Secure the clutch assembly using a clutch
holder tool.
• Use the special tool to loosen and remove the
clutch nut along with the washer.
• Carefully lift out the clutch stack (plates and
discs).
NOTES :- 270
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Remove the spline washer and clutch housing.
• Inspect the bearings for smooth rotation, if
rough or worn.
• Gently pull out the water pump drive gear.
• Check for damaged or worn teeth—replace if
necessary.
• Secure the GPD gear with a special holding tool.
• Use the appropriate wrench or socket to
remove the nut and washer.
• Carefully slide out the GPD gear.
!! CAUTION
• Use a Cloth for Protection:
• When removing gears, place a cloth between the screwdriver and components to
prevent scratches.
• Avoid direct metal-to-metal contact to protect delicate surfaces.
NOTES :- 271
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Prevent Damage to the Crankcase Matching
Surface:
• While removing the coolant gear, ensure that
no forceful contact damages the crankcase.
• Use proper tools and gentle techniques
to avoid scratching the mating surfaces.
• Carefully slide out the GPD gear along
with its key.
• Keep the key safely to prevent misplacement.
• Gently pull out the oil pump drive gear from its
mounting.
• Ensure that the gear is not forced
out, preventing damage to the
crankcase.
• Use an 8 mm T-spanner to loosen and remove
the oil pump mounting bolts.
• Carefully detach the oil pump assembly from
the crankcase.
NOTES :- 272
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Carefully extract the dowel pin from the oil
pump housing.
• Inspect for wear, deformation, or damage -
replace if necessary.
• Use an Allen wrench to loosen and remove the
M6 Allen bolt securing the slack side chain
guide.
• Gently detach the chain guide to avoid
damaging adjacent components.
• Carefully slide the silent timing chain off the
sprockets.
• Ensure no forceful pulling to prevent damage.
NOTES :- 273
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Locate the secondary oil filter cover.
• Gently pull out the oil filter from its housing.
• Check for clogs, debris, or damage—replace if
dirty or worn.
• Clean the filter housing before reinstalling a
new filter.
• Use an 8 mm T-spanner to loosen and remove
the bolts securing the gear shifter assembly.
• Identify and remove the 8 mm bolt securing
additional parts of the assembly.
• Carefully take out the gear shifter washer
without dropping it into the engine casing.
NOTES :- 274
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Gently pull out the entire gear shifter assembly
from its mounting.
• Ensure no damage to the surrounding
components during removal.
• Verify that the cylinder block gasket has been
completely removed during the block removal
process.
• Ensure no gasket residue remains on the mating
surfaces.
• Clean the surface properly before reassembly
to prevent leaks.
• Gradually loosen the bolts securing the right
hand side (RHS) crankcase.
NOTES :- 275
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Use an 8 mm socket spanner to gradually loosen
the bolts in a crisscross pattern to prevent
uneven stress.
• Remove the following Hex Flanged Bolts:
• 3 Nos. - M6 x 60 mm
• 2 Nos. - M6 x 35 mm
• 1 Nos. - M6 x 65 mm
• 3 Nos. - M6 x 75 mm
• 2 Nos. - M6 x 85 mm
• Gently separate the LHS crankcase from the engine assembly.
• Use a rubber mallet if needed to tap lightly -
avoid using metal tools to prevent damage.
• Identify the timing marks on the balancer shaft
gear and the crankshaft gear.
• The marks are usually small dot / lines stamped
on the gear teeth.
• Rotate the crankshaft and balancer shaft so
that their timing marks line up correctly.
• Ensure the marks are precisely aligned.
Misalignment can cause engine vibration and
imbalance.
• Carefully slide out the balancer
shaft from its housing.
• Ensure the timing marks are noted
before removal for proper
reassembly.
NOTES :- 276
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Gently pull out the crankshaft from the
crankcase.
• Ensure that the balancer shaft is fully
disengaged before removing the crankshaft.
Ball Bearing Ring removal
• Secure the Special tool properly around the ball
bearing ring to ensure even
pressure.
• Gradually apply force to extract
the bearing ring without
damaging the crankcase or
surrounding components.
• Once loosened, carefully remove the bearing
ring by hand or with pliers.
Ball Bearing Ring Installation
• Clean the bearing seat to remove any dirt,
debris, or old grease.
• Lightly lubricate the bearing seat with engine oil
or grease for smoother installation.
NOTES :- 277
3.4.1 BLOCK AND PISTON ASSEMBLY
• Align the ball bearing ring with its housing.
• Use a mallet & tool to fit the bearing ring into

i. Install Oil Ring
Oil ring has 3 Layers- Upper, middle and Lower.

middle) approx. 23° & should not cross molycoat

• OD Specification: 16.994 - 17.000 mm

NOTES :- 296
33..14.4.2 OPIILS DTORNA IANNINDG R &IN OGISL FILTER REPLACEMENT
4. TROUBLESHOOTING
i. Loss of Compression:
a. Symptoms
• Reduced engine power, excessive exhaust smoke, poor performance.
b. Troubleshooting:
• Perform a compression test to determine if there is a loss of compression in
cylinders.
• If compression is low, inspect the piston rings for damage, wear, or improper
installation.
• Replace worn or damaged rings with the appropriate size and type.
ii. Excessive Blow-By
a. Symptoms
• Oil or exhaust gas escaping past the piston rings into the crankcase.
b. Troubleshooting:
• Inspect the piston rings for damage, wear, or improper seating.
• Replace worn or damaged rings and ensure they are correctly installed.
iii. Excessive Oil Consumption:
a. Symptoms
• Frequent need to top up engine oil, blue smoke from the exhaust.
b. Troubleshooting:
• Check for leaks or external oil sources.
• Perform a wet and dry compression test to determine if the oil is being burned
due to worn rings.
• If the wet compression test shows a significant increase in pressure, it indicates
worn piston rings.
iv. Ring Sticking or Carbon Buildup:
a. Symptoms
• Loss of power, poor fuel economy, smoking exhaust.
NOTES :- 297
33..14.4.2 OPIILS DTORNA IANNINDG R &IN OGISL FILTER REPLACEMENT
b. Troubleshooting:
• Remove the cylinder head and piston to inspect the rings.
• Examine the rings for carbon buildup or sticking to the piston groove.
• Clean the rings and grooves using an appropriate cleaner and a ring groove
cleaning tool.
• Replace any damaged or excessively worn rings.
v. Ring End Gap Issues:
a. Symptoms
• Uneven compression, poor performance, engine knocking.
b. Troubleshooting:
• Measure the end gap of the piston rings with a feeler gauge to ensure they are
within the specifications.
• If the end gap is too small, the rings may expand and seize. If it's too large, it can
lead to poor compression.
• Replace rings with the correct end gap specifications as specified in the service
manual.
vi. Incorrect Ring Installation:
a. Symptoms
• Poor compression, loss of power, engine noise.
b. Troubleshooting:
• Ensure the piston rings are correctly installed with the proper orientation and
gaps.
• Disassemble and reassemble the rings correctly if they were installed improperly.
NOTES :- 298
33..15. 4R OOTILO DRR AASISNEINMGB &LY O/ISLT FAIRLTTEERR RCELUPLTACCHEMENT
1. INTRODUCTION
2. TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS
3. LOCATION
4. STARTER MOTOR & FREE WHEEL COMPONENTS
5. DISASSEMBLY OF STARTER MOTOR
6. ASSEMBLY OF STARTER MOTOR
7. TROUBLESHOOTING
NOTES :- 299
33..15. 4R OOTILO DRR AASISNEINMGB &LY O/ISLT FAIRLTTEERR RCELUPLTACCHEMENT
1. INTRODUCTION
Here's a general overview of how an electric starter motor works in a motorcycle:
i. Starter Motor:
The electric starter motor is typically located on the side of the engine. It is a
small electric motor powered by the motorcycle's battery.
ii. Battery:
The motorcycle's battery supplies the electrical power needed to turn the starter
motor. It must be in good condition to provide sufficient power.
iii. Starter Button:
To start the motorcycle, the rider usually pushes a starter button on the
handlebars, which sends an electrical signal to the starter motor.
iv. Solenoid:
In many motorcycles, a solenoid is used to engage the starter motor with the
engine's flywheel. When the starter button is pressed, the solenoid creates a
magnetic field that moves a plunger, connecting the starter motor to the engine.
v. Starter Motor Operation:
Once engaged, the starter motor spins a gear, which meshes with the
motorcycle's engine's flywheel or starter clutch. This rotation turns the engine
over, initiating the internal combustion process.
vi. Engine Starting:
As the starter motor turns the engine, the spark plug ignites the fuel-air mixture,
and the engine comes to life. Once the engine is running, the rider can release
the starter button.
2. TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION
• Starter driven gear - (19/69T)
• Driven type – Electric driven
• Voltage- 12V
• Peak Power Output- 0.7 kW
NOTES :- 300
33..15. 4R OOTILO DRR AASISNEINMGB &LY O/ISLT FAIRLTTEERR RCELUPLTACCHEMENT
3. LOCATION IN VEHICLE
4. STARTER MOTOR & FREE WHEEL COMPONENTS
